etymology: From Thor — the Norse god of thunder, strength, and storms, wielder of the hammer Mjolnir. Berzelius, a Swede, chose the name from Norse mythology to honor the Scandinavian tradition. An earlier 'thorium' had been named by Berzelius himself in 1815 for a different mineral, which later proved to be impure yttrium phosphate — so the name was re-used for the genuine new element in 1829.

timeline.event: Berzelius analyzes a mineral from the Falun copper mine and believes he has found a new element — he names it 'thorium' after Thor. Further investigation proves the mineral is yttrium phosphate contaminated with silicate. Berzelius withdraws the name but saves it for future use.

Morten Thrane Esmark, a Norwegian priest and amateur mineralogist (and son of a famous Norwegian mineralogist), finds an unusual black mineral on the island of Løvøya in Norway. He sends samples to his father, who sends them to Berzelius.

Berzelius analyzes the Norwegian mineral and finds it contains a new earth unlike anything previously known. He isolates a tin-white metallic powder and names it thorium — recycling the name he had withdrawn in 1815. The mineral is named thorite in honor of the discovery.

Marie Curie and Gerhard Carl Schmidt independently discover that thorium is radioactive — the second element found to be naturally radioactive, after uranium. Schmidt publishes his observation first by weeks; Marie Curie publishes independently. The discovery that thorium decays radioactively is fundamental to understanding radioactive decay series.

Carl Auer von Welsbach uses thorium oxide in his incandescent gas mantle — a fabric coated with 99% ThO₂ and 1% CeO₂ that glows brilliantly white when heated. The Welsbach mantle transforms gas lighting and makes thorium one of the most commercially significant radioactive materials of the early 20th century.

The Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) and the Shippingport Atomic Power Station's light water breeder reactor program begin exploring thorium as a nuclear fuel — thorium-232 absorbs neutrons to become uranium-233, a fissile fuel. India begins its three-stage nuclear program explicitly designed around its large thorium reserves.

Interest in thorium reactors surges following the Fukushima disaster — thorium fuel cycles produce less long-lived transuranic waste and are considered proliferation-resistant. Several nations and companies announce thorium reactor programs, though commercial thorium power remains unrealized.

story: Berzelius named thorium twice — once for a mineral that turned out to be something else entirely, and once for a genuine new element. His persistence in using the same name is one of chemistry's minor curiosities. Thorium's history splits neatly into two eras: before and after the discovery of radioactivity. Before 1898, thorium was an unremarkable rare earth. After 1898, it became one of only a handful of naturally occurring radioactive elements, and its decay chain includes radon (the indoor air hazard) and radium. The gas mantle — one of the most widely used thorium applications of the 20th century — placed mildly radioactive thorium oxide in billions of camping lanterns and gas lights worldwide. Thorium's potential as a nuclear fuel remains one of the energy debates of the 21st century.
source_note: Discovered by Berzelius (1829) in Norwegian thorite mineral. Named after Thor (Norse god). Radioactivity discovered by Schmidt and Curie (1898). Published in Annalen der Physik und Chemie, 1829.
